title: UK recyclability labels explained
subtitle: What the symbols on packaging mean, and what actually gets collected
excerpt: The widely recycled, check locally, and not currently recycled labels on UK packaging indicate national collection rates. Above 75% of local authorities collect a material: widely recycled. 20-75%: check locally. Below 20%: not currently recycled. The label tells you about collection infrastructure, not about whether the material is technically recyclable.
sourceNotes: OPRL (On-Pack Recycling Label) scheme guidance and label definitions. WRAP Recycle Now label guidance. Defra guidance on Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) and packaging recyclability in England.

glossaryTerms.definition: On-Pack Recycling Label scheme. A UK industry scheme run by WRAP that standardises recycling instructions on packaging. Labels use three categories: Widely Recycled (collected by 75%+ of local authorities), Check Locally (20-75%), and Not Currently Recycled (below 20%). Adopted by most major UK retailers and food brands.

Non-recyclable material mixed into a recyclables collection. Common sources: food residue on packaging, non-recyclable plastics mixed into paper collections, liquids in glass. High contamination causes whole loads to be rejected and sent to landfill or incineration, negating the benefit of separation at home.

content.text: label system (on most UK food packaging) provides a nationally consistent guide. The three-category system tells you what most councils collect. Your council may collect more or fewer materials than the national average: check your council's recycling page for the definitive list, especially for plastic types, cartons, and small metals.
Widely recycled: collected at the kerbside by 75%+ of UK local authorities. Includes most rigid plastics (bottles and tubs), steel and aluminium cans, cardboard, glass bottles and jars, and paper.

Check locally: collected by 20-75% of councils. Includes aerosol cans, plastic bags and film (some councils), cartons (Tetra Pak), and mixed-material packaging. Check your council's website to confirm.

Not currently recycled: collected by fewer than 20% of councils. Includes black plastic trays (cannot be detected by some sorting equipment), mixed-laminate pouches, crisp packets, and most flexible plastics. These go in the general waste bin.
A light rinse (not a full wash) is sufficient for most recyclable packaging.

from food residue is a significant cause of recycling load rejection. Yoghurt pots, sauce bottles, and tins with significant food residue left inside contaminate the paper and cardboard they are mixed with during transport. A quick rinse under residual washing-up water is sufficient: no need to use fresh hot water and detergent.
Wish-cycling: putting non-recyclable items in the recycling bin in the hope they will be processed. They will not: they contaminate the load.
Bagging recyclables in plastic bags: bags jam sorting machinery. Unless your council specifies bag collection, put items loose in the bin.
Shredded paper in the recycling bin: the short fibres contaminate the paper stream. Bag shredded paper in a paper bag or compost it.
